title: practical english usage - Part 171
content:
I like the salad, but I don't like the soup, (     I like not the soup.) Do is normally used if there is no other auxiliary verb.

We have not forgotten you.  it was not raining. She can't swim.

We make negative verb forms by putting not after an auxiliary verb.

1  negative       verb    forms:    auxiliary      + not 217 negative       structures:      basic    rules For negative questions,  ►    2)8.   For ellipsis in questions (e.g. Seen Jack? Coming tonight?), ►    277.

Who did you buy the ticket from? What did you clean the floor with?

What are you talking about? (     About what are you talking?) objects. (For details, ►    209.) Prepositions often come at the end of wh-questions, separated from their 8  prepositions:        What      are    you     talking        about?

Basic Clause Types    Section  19 grammar •  218   negative questions Have they not at least a room to stay in?  Is Mrs Allen not at home?

Hasn't she any friends to help her?     Aren't you ready?

auxiliary verbs.

Non-auxiliary have (in British English) and be go in the same position as Does she not understand? Why have you not reserved the flight yet?

auxiliary verb + subject + not...

Doesn't she understand? Why haven't you reserved your flight yet?

auxiliary verb + n't + subject...

(Uncontracted negative questions are usually formal.) Contracted and uncontracted negative questions have different word order.

1  structure:      Doesn't      she    understand?            / Does     she    not    understand?

218  negative       questions            __ I've found some mushrooms.  I haven't found any mushrooms.

the 'non-affirmative' words any, anybody, etc (►  222). Compare: We   do not usually use some, somebody, etc in negative clauses. Instead, we  use 7 'non-affirmative'          words:    any,    etc He has few reasons for staying has he?

There's little point in doing anything about it, is there? (     .. . isn't there?) The  same thing happens after clauses with little and few (►    168).

She never smiled, did she?

You seldom work on Saturdays, do you? (       sel(tom ivork-en-SntHfday^; You don't work on Sundays, do you?

After negative clauses, question tags (►  305-306) are not negative.

6 question       tags:   You     don't    ..., do   you?

/ never did like her.

However, do can be used for emphasis or contrast.

The soup lacks salt. We're unhappy about your decision.

He seldom / rarely / hardly ever works. They refuse to listen to reason.

He never works, (     He dees never work.) He doesn't work.

We   do not normally use the auxiliary do with these other words. Compare: He's seldom / rarely / hardly ever at home.

He's not at home.  He's never at home.

Other words besides not can make a clause negative. Compare: 5  other     negative       words:    never,      seldom,        etc grammar *218   negative questions Don't you ever listen to what I say?

selfish to lend me ...?') Can't you lend me your pen for a minute? (= something like 'Are you too Negative questions may be understood as complaints or criticisms.

Can't you help me for a moment?

common in informal requests) You can't help me for a moment, can you? (negative statement + question tag, request) Excuse me, can you help me for a moment? (ordinary question, used as a question tag (especially British English).

things. This is done with ordinary questions, or with negative statement + But in other cases we  do not usually use negative questions to ask people to do Why don't you shut up?

Why don't you ...? can also introduce aggressive suggestions.

Why don't you come and spend the weekend with us?

Wouldn't you like something to drink?

Won't you come in for a few minutes?

Pressing invitations and offers often begin Won't you . ..?, Wouldn't you ...? or 3   polite     requests,      invitations,        offers,    complaints        and   criticisms Didn't the alarm go off? I wonder what's wrong with it.

Hasn't the mail come yet?

something has not happened or is not happening.

This kind of negative question can show that the speaker is surprised that Oh,  dear. Can't they come this evening?
